coredata.dc:title: Preparation, regulatory mechanism, and full-spectrum fluorescence synthesis of ginkgo-derived trichromatic carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Changge
Guo, Yuxiang
Mao, Dongxu
Li, Yubao
Li, Tian
Li, Ren’ai
Wang, Qi
coredata.dc:description: Biomass is a sustainable and eco-friendly resource with great potential as a precursor for synthesizing high-value carbon dots (CDs). This research synthesized full-spectrum fluorescence CDs using a microwave method with ginkgo leaves. The blue, green, and red fluorescent CDs have the following color coordinates according to the International Commission on Illumination (CIE): (0.18, 0.19), (0.32, 0.47), and (0.48, 0.29), respectively, with corresponding fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of 13.07, 6.09, and 6.72 %. The trichromatic CDs were comprehensively characterized to explore their properties and reveal the regulatory mechanisms responsible for the differences in their fluorescence behavior. Further investigations demonstrated that precise tuning of the mixing ratios of the trichromatic CDs enabled the realization of a full-spectrum fluorescence display ranging from blue to red. Additionally, the trichromatic CDs were effectively used in fluorescent inks and polyvinyl alcohol fluorescent films, achieving a novel fluorescent anti-counterfeiting effect. In conclusion, this work offers a green synthesis method for turning biomass into trichromatic CDs, addressing the complex challenge of fluorescent color modulation in CDs and providing valuable insights into potential applications and the full-spectrum fluorescence display of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ginkgo leaves
Trichromatic
Regulatory mechanism
Full-spectrum fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and morphological properties of CDs
Full spectrum fluorescent color synthesis of CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (BK20240685).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of ginkgo-derived trichromatic CDs preparation and application.
UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b), and R-CDs (c); photoluminescence spectrum of B-CDs (d), G-CDs (e), and R-CDs (f).
HRTEM photographs of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b), and R-CDs (c); particle size histogram of B-CDs (d), G-CDs (e), and R-CDs (f); XRD patterns of B-CDs (g), G-CDs (h), and R-CDs (i).
FTIR spectrum (a) and complete XPS spectra (b) of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs; High-resolution C1s spectra for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs (c-e); High-resolution O1s spectra for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs(f-h).
Lifetime decay of B-CDs(a), G-CDs(b), and R-CDs(c).
Schematic illustration of the band gap energies of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs.

Three primary color mixing principle diagrams (a). CIE coordinates of white fluorescent CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs(b). Full spectrum fluorescent CDs solutions (c). Multicolor PVA fluorescent film(d). Fluorescence patterns under daylight and UV light (e).
Content of elements C, N, O, and Zn in B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs.
The Chemical bonds content of C in B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs.

head.ce:title: Preparation, regulatory mechanism, and full-spectrum fluorescence synthesis of ginkgo-derived trichromatic carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass is a sustainable and eco-friendly resource with great potential as a precursor for synthesizing high-value carbon dots (CDs). This research synthesized full-spectrum fluorescence CDs using a microwave method with ginkgo leaves. The blue, green, and red fluorescent CDs have the following color coordinates according to the International Commission on Illumination (CIE): (0.18, 0.19), (0.32, 0.47), and (0.48, 0.29), respectively, with corresponding fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of 13.07, 6.09, and 6.72 %. The trichromatic CDs were comprehensively characterized to explore their properties and reveal the regulatory mechanisms responsible for the differences in their fluorescence behavior. Further investigations demonstrated that precise tuning of the mixing ratios of the trichromatic CDs enabled the realization of a full-spectrum fluorescence display ranging from blue to red. Additionally, the trichromatic CDs were effectively used in fluorescent inks and polyvinyl alcohol fluorescent films, achieving a novel fluorescent anti-counterfeiting effect. In conclusion, this work offers a green synthesis method for turning biomass into trichromatic CDs, addressing the complex challenge of fluorescent color modulation in CDs and providing valuable insights into potential applications and the full-spectrum fluorescence display of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional, carbon-based nanomaterials composed of quasi-spherical carbon particles [1]. Typically, the structure of CDs is either amorphous or exhibits partial crystallinity. The unique characteristics of CDs include their low toxicity [2], strong fluorescence properties [3], and excellent biological compatibility [4], which render them extremely promising for various applications, including bioimaging [5], anti-counterfeiting [6,7], fluorescent sensing [8], and optoelectronic devices [9]. There are two general techniques for CD synthesis: the top-down approach and the bottom-up approach [10]. The top-down approach entails converting bigger carbon materials, such as carbon nanofibers, carbon nanotubes, and large graphene sheets, into CDs via physical or chemical processes. In contrast, the bottom-up approach generates CDs by polymerizing and carbonizing smaller molecules or polymers. Both methods can be implemented using various techniques, including microwave method [11], pyrolytic carbonization [12], hydrothermal treatment [13], laser ablation [14], and template method [15]. Among these, the microwave method stands out due to its advantages, such as rapid, easy, and uniform heating, making it a more efficient approach compared to others.

CDs fall into two main categories depending on their carbon source: biomass-based CDs and non-biomass-based CDs. Non-biomass-based CDs are typically synthesized from chemical reagents, such as ethylenediamine, o-Phenylenediamine [16], and cysteine [17], while biomass-based CDs are derived from natural organic materials [18], including grape leaves [19], pork [20], and food waste [21]. As a renewable resource, biomass has several benefits, such as affordability, environmental sustainability, and wide availability. Therefore, converting these low-value biomass materials into high-value CDs is important. In 2012, Zhou et al. synthesized CDs with a QY of approximately 7.1 % through the pyrolytic carbonization of watermelon rinds. The resulting biomass-derived CDs exhibited high water solubility and luminescence efficiency, making them suitable for cellular fluorescence imaging [22]. Song et al. synthesized CDs rich in amide, carboxyl, and amino groups using natural wool keratin as the carbon source. These surface functionalities facilitated interactions with metal ions like iron and chromium, leading to fluorescence quenching, which enabled rapid ion detection [23]. Kumawat et al. synthesized mango leaf-derived graphene quantum dots via microwave-assisted synthesis. The CDs exhibited bright red fluorescence under UV light and demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, selectively localizing within the cytoplasm, making them suitable for bioimaging applications [24]. Liu et al. reported for the first time a deep red fluorescent CD from mulberry leaves with a QY of 73 % and an emission peak at 676 nm [25]. Notably, silkworms fed with these CDs produced silk fibers that retained the red fluorescence, demonstrating the potential for biologically integrated applications. Biomass-based CDs retain the superior properties of traditional CDs and offer enhanced biocompatibility and sustainability, owing to their natural origin. This highlights their promising potential for environmentally friendly and bio-compatible applications [26].

Fluorescence is a key advantage of biomass-based CDs. The fluorescence properties of biomass-based CDs are influenced by factors such as their size, shape, surface modifications, and fluorescence emission mechanisms, all of which vary depending on the preparation method. The ability of biomass-based CDs to emit multicolor fluorescence presents distinct advantages for applications in high-sensitivity detection, medical imaging, and anti-counterfeiting. However, current research on biomass-based CDs has primarily focused on the development of monochromatic fluorescence, which limits their potential for broader practical applications [27]. Multicolor fluorescence emission can be realized by optimizing the preparation conditions and synthesis methods of biomass-based CDs, which can significantly expand its versatility, improve its commercial value and meet a wide range of application needs.

Ginkgo, a “living fossil” with a history traceable over hundreds of millions of years, is a plant species native to China. The chemical composition of ginkgo leaves is complex, comprising organic compounds such as proteins, cellulose, flavonoids, terpene lactones, phenolic acids, and non-structural carbohydrates. These components are readily converted into CDs under microwave radiation. In this paper, we devised an environmentally friendly and simple synthetic approach to prepare blue, green, and red CDs through an efficient microwave-assisted method. Ginkgo leaves, commonly found in Nanjing, were used as the carbon precursor, while urea, zinc acetate dihydrate, and NaOH served as dopants (Fig. 1 ). Additionally, the mechanism behind the fluorescence color variation was investigated. A full-spectrum fluorescence display was achieved by adjusting the mixing ratios of the prepared trichromatic CDs. The resulting CDs demonstrate potential applications in anti-counterfeiting technologies, such as fluorescent inks and PVA fluorescent films. This work offers a novel strategy for the green and sustainable synthesis of biomass-derived, multicolor fluorescent CDs, broadening their applicability and commercial potential.

Fresh mature ginkgo leaves were obtained by harvesting from the campus of Nanjing Forestry University in China, between May and November. Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. supplied urea, zinc acetate dihydrate, NaOH, and polyvinyl alcohol(PVA). Ultrapure water was used in the experiment.

First, fresh ginkgo leaves were washed with ultrapure water and then dried to remove surface moisture. The dried ginkgo leaves were then ground into a paste. 4 g of ginkgo leaves and 2 g of urea were added to 20 mL of ultrapure water. The resulting mixture was subjected to microwave irradiation (Galanz, C2AW-GF3, China) for 7 min at 720 W. After cooling to room temperature, additional ultrapure water was added, and the mixture was sonicated for 20 min. The solution was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Subsequently, the filtered solution was dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da. Finally, place in the freeze dryer to get blue fluorescence CDs(B-CDs).

4 g of ginkgo leaves were combined with 0.7 g of zinc acetate dehydrate and 20 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was subjected to microwave irradiation at 720 W for 7 min. Subsequently, it performed the same purification process as B-CDs. Finally, it was placed in the freeze dryer to get green fluorescence CDs(G-CDs).

4 g of ginkgo leaves were combined with 0.5 g of NaOH and 20 mL of ultrapure water to synthesize red fluorescence CDs (R-CDs). The mixture was irradiated in a 900 W microwave oven for 10 min. Subsequently, the same purification process was performed for B-CDs. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain R-CDs.

The UV–Vis spectra were conducted using a Lambda 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured by an Edinburgh FS5 spectrometer. CDs morphology was characterized using a JEM-1400 high-resolution transmission electron microscope. The crystal properties were measured by Ultima IV X-ray diffractometry (XRD). The surface chemistry properties were determined using a VERTEX 80 V Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR). An AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer was used to evaluate X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The absolute QY and fluorescence lifetime were tested using a FluoroMax-4 steady-state/lifetime spectrofluorometer.

First, 3 g of PVA was added to 30 mL of water and stirred at 90 °C until completely dissolved, then 2 mL of each of the B-CDs (1.5 mg/mL), G-CDs (2 mg/mL), and R-CDs (2 mg/mL) solutions were individually combined with 2 mL of the PVA liquid. The mixture was stirred for 10 min to ensure homogeneous blending, followed by sonication for 10 min to remove air bubbles. The resulting mixture was then dropwise added into a sterile petri dish. The petri dish was left in a dry, dust-free environment for 24 h to allow for film formation. Finally, the PVA fluorescent films were obtained.

B-CDs solution (1.5 mg/mL), G-CDs solution (2 mg/mL), and R-CDs (2 mg/mL) were administered as anti-counterfeit fluorescent inks into an empty ink cartridge of a Canon printer and then printed onto non-fluorescent paper.

In the preparation of trichromatic CDs, too low microwave power and too short time cannot provide enough energy to drive the full pyrolysis or oxidation of ginkgo leaves, while too high microwave power and too long time will cause excessive carbonization of CDs and even lead to fluorescence quenching of CDs. The amount of dopant determines the fluorescence wavelength and intensity of CDs, and trichromatic CDs investigated in this work were synthesized under systematically optimized reaction conditions to ensure maximal photoluminescence efficiency.

Fig. 2a-c presents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs. All CDs exhibit prominent absorption peaks in the range of 250–300 nm, which are mainly caused by π-π* transitions in CC bonds [28]. Besides, G-CDs and R-CDs display weak absorption peaks within the 300–650 nm range, which can be ascribed to n-π* transitions of CC or CN bonds within their structures [29]. The inset images show that B-CDs and R-CDs solutions appear brown under sunlight, whereas the G-CDs solution is nearly colorless. Under UV light, B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs emit blue, green, and red fluorescence, respectively. Fig. 2d-f shows the PL spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs. Both B-CDs and G-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission characteristics, which are probably caused by the distribution of various functional groups [30]. For B-CDs, the maximum fluorescence intensity occurs under excitation at 380 nm, with the emission peak at 460 nm. For G-CDs, the fluorescence intensity is highest under excitation at 360 nm, with the emission peak at 508 nm. R-CDs exhibit dual fluorescence emission peaks: one in the range of 400–550 nm, which shows clear excitation dependence, and another at approximately 658 nm, which is excitation-independent. The interaction between the surface functional groups and the carbon core of R-CDs may contribute to this unique behavior [31]. Furthermore, QY of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were measured using transient steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, yielding values of 13.07, 6.09, and 6.72 %, respectively. In comparison, Zhang et al. prepared B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs from waste leather scrap, the QY was 3.6 %, 5.14 %, and 1.05 %, respectively [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were investigated using HRTEM and XRD, with the corresponding HRTEM photographs, size histogram, and XRD patterns shown in Fig. 3 . Three types of CDs are spherical and have good dispersion, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of the graphite carbon lattice. The average particle sizes of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs are 3.98, 4.23, and 4.52 nm, respectively. The XRD diffraction patterns demonstrated that G-CDs exhibited two distinct diffraction peaks around 26° and 44°, while B-CDs and R-CDs exhibited a wide diffraction peak between 21° and 23°. These broad diffraction peaks indicate the presence of disordered structures in all three types of CDs [33]. This structural disorder is likely due to the incomplete carbonization of the precursors during microwave irradiation.

FTIR was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of the three types of CDs (Fig. 4a). All three CDs show peaks at 3410 cm-1 and 3212 cm-1 attributed to the vibrations of the -OH and NH bonds, respectively. The CH stretching vibration is detected at 2933 cm−1, whereas the vibrational peaks at 1656 cm-1 and 1600 cm-1 represent the stretching vibrations of CO and CC bonds, respectively. Additionally, the prominent peaks at 1411 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of the CO bond [34]. It is noteworthy that the signal intensities of the CO and –OH bonds of G-CDs are reduced compared with those of B-CDs and R-CDs, which may be due to the chemical binding of zinc ions to the carboxylic acid groups on the G-CDs surface, thereby partially masking the vibrational characteristics of these groups [35].

XPS was used to further explore the elemental composition and structural properties of the three types of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the XPS spectra of all three CDs exhibited peaks for C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. Additionally, the Zn 2p signal was detected in the XPS spectra of G-CDs. The composition of the elemental in three CDs was further analyzed in Table 1 , the Zn content in G-CDs was found to be 3.62 %, confirming the successful incorporation of Zn ions into G-CDs. Additionally, among G-CDs and R-CDs, which were not externally doped with nitrogen, R-CDs exhibited a higher level of self-doped nitrogen than G-CDs. This difference can be attributed to the higher microwave irradiation time and power applied during the synthesis of R-CDs, as well as the alkaline environment created by NaOH doping, which facilitated the dehydration, condensation, and carbonization of ginkgo leaves, thereby increasing the nitrogen content from self-doping [36].

The deconvoluted XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s for the three types of CDs are presented in Fig. 4c-h. The C 1 s spectra are resolved into three peaks at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.6 eV, which correspond to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N, and CO, respectively [37]. The O 1 s spectra are fitted with two peaks at 531.7 and 533.2 eV, attributed to CO and CO, respectively. As shown in Table 2 , the relative content of the C-C/C=C bond, representing the sp2 conjugated domain, increases from 36.24 % in B-CDs to 60.75 % in R-CDs. Moreover, from B-CDs to R-CDs, the ratio of sp2 (C-C/C=C) to sp3 (C-O/C-N) increases from 0.82 to 2.03, and the sp3 hybrid carbons in the CDs are more transformed into sp2 conjugated domains [38]. The sp2 hybridized carbon can form a continuous conjugated system in CDs, which makes the energy required for the electron leaps of CDs lower, and thus higher sp2 hybridized structural domains can lower the band gap of the HOMO-LUMO energy level, leading to the fluorescence redshift of CDs [39]. Fluorescence decay curves for the three CDs were fitted (Fig. 5a-c). The fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs consists solely of τ1, which is associated with the carbon core. In contrast, the fluorescence lifetimes of G-CDs and R-CDs are composed of both τ1 and τ2, indicating that their fluorescence emission arises from radiative transitions between energy levels associated with both the carbon core and surface defect states [40]. From B-CDs to R-CDs, the proportion of τ2 gradually increases, suggesting that the fluorescence emission from surface defect states becomes progressively more dominant. The average fluorescence lifetimes of the three CDs were found to be 4.00, 7.72, and 2.22 ns, respectively. The enhanced surface passivation of G-CDs, attributed to Zn ion doping, results in longer fluorescence lifetimes, as the surface passivation flaws serve as new excitation energy traps [41].

For B-CDs, urea can be used as a nitrogen source to introduce N atoms into CDs through microwave reaction, replacing some of the C atoms in the carbon skeleton of CDs. The amount of urea added determines the amount of N element doping in B-CDs, which is closely related to the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs, but excessive urea may not be involved in the reaction but is mostly decomposed into gases in microwave radiation. N atoms doped into the structure of CDs can be used as electron acceptors or donors, thus changing the electronic structure of CDs; this change can generate new fluorescence emission pathways [42]. In addition, N atoms can fill the dangling bonds on the CDs surface, reducing the non-radiative energy dissipation pathways and thus increasing the fluorescence QY, which accounts for the highest QY observed in B-CDs. In the case of G-CDs, Zn2+ was introduced due to the addition of zinc acetate dihydrate. During the formation of G-CDs, doping of Zn ions catalyzes the CDs carbonation process and increases the proportion of sp2 conjugated domains, while concurrently, Zn2+ combines with -COOH or -OH groups on the surface of CDs to form a stable ZnO coordination structure and reduce surface energy; the FTIR of G-CDs confirmed this argument. Therefore, the doping of Zn ions enhances the surface passivation of G-CDs, introducing new surface states or defect energy levels. Furthermore, the incorporation of Zn ions is responsible for the formation of unsaturated carbon bonds or carbon vacancies, which enlarges the sp2 conjugated structural domain. These structural changes alter the surface states and the electronic structure of the G-CDs, leading to the formation of new energy levels. The HOMO-LUMO band gap of G-CDs is reduced, and the energy required for the electron jump is lowered, leading to a redshift in the emission wavelength and resulting in the characteristic green fluorescence emission of G-CDs [35]. In comparison to G-CDs and B-CDs, the synthesis of R-CDs involves higher microwave power and longer reaction times. The addition of NaOH increased the pH of the reaction system during the formation of CDs, which increased the carbonization of CDs, and the appropriate alkaline environment provided is important to the development of the sp2 conjugated structural domain in R-CDs. As a result, the degree of graphitization of R-CDs is the highest among the three CDs. Furthermore, this alkaline environment accelerated the cross-linking polymerization reaction of ginkgo leaves under microwave energy irradiation, which promoted the formation of carbon nuclear and maintained the blue emission inherent in the sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon nuclear. After which the remaining portions, such as incompletely carbonylated functional groups including -NH₂, -COOH, and -OH, or polymer chain residues, were enriched at the surface of the CDs to form additional subfluorescent groups, constituting the surface states fluorescence emission, allowing the R-CDs to form a core-shell heterostructure that permits different electron transition paths [43]. Consequently, R-CDs exhibit dual fluorescence emission peaks originating from both the carbon core and surface states, corresponding to the multimodal fluorescence emission of R-CDs. In summary, the fluorescence emission of trichromatic CDs is determined by the interaction of the sp2 conjugated structural domain and surface states.

To verify this hypothesis, the band gap energies of the three types of CDs were calculated using the formula.
E
g
opt
= 1240 /
λ edge
[44], and a schematic diagram was designed(Fig. 6 ). B-CDs exhibit a band gap of 3.4 eV. For G-CDs, the introduction of Zn ions alters the radiative complex of surface-trapped electrons and holes, resulting in the development of a new HOMO energy level and a reduction in the band gap to 2.9 eV. In the case of R-CDs, the band gap of the carbon core is 3.4 eV, while the addition of external fluorescent groups leads to the creation of new surface states, lowering the HOMO level band gap to 1.7 eV. Upon UV light absorption, a portion of the excitons in the carbon core radiatively recombine, producing blue fluorescence, while the remaining excitons undergo non-radiative decay and transfer to the surface states level of energy, causing the emission of red fluorescence [45]. The fluorescence emission from the surface states of R-CDs dominates over that from the carbon core, thereby contributing to the predominant red fluorescence observed in R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Full spectrum fluorescent color synthesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence follows the principle of additive color mixing, as illustrated in Fig. 7a. Blue, green, and red light can be obtained by mixing cyan, yellow, and magenta light, respectively, with the combined mixture producing white light. Based on calculations, the CIE chromaticity coordinates for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs are (0.18, 0.19), (0.32, 0.47), and (0.48, 0.29), respectively. By mixing the three types of CDs in a ratio of 5:11:8, the resulting CIE coordinates are (0.35, 0.37), corresponding to a white light emission, as shown in the inset, which displays the white fluorescence of the mixed CDs solutions under UV light (Fig. 7b). As depicted in Fig. 7c, the solutions labeled “R”, “B” and “G” represent the pure R-CDs, B-CDs, and G-CDs solutions, respectively. When mixed in specific proportions, the resulting CDs solution produces a full-spectrum fluorescence display, the specific proportions are shown in Table S1.

PVA was selected as the film substrate to incorporate the CDs solution, as shown in Fig. 7d, with the resulting films exhibiting distinct fluorescent colors upon irradiation with UV light. The mixing ratio of PVA fluorescent films are shown in Table S2. Furthermore, solutions of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were loaded into a printer cartridge, and the patterns of a ginkgo leaves icon, the English word “Ginkgo”, and the Chinese characters for “Ginkgo” were printed onto non-fluorescent paper. As illustrated in Fig. 7e, under UV light, fluorescent patterns in blue, green, and red could be observed. Additionally, an anti-counterfeiting strategy was developed, in which the nonfluorescent paper displays an incorrect black numerical pattern “888,888” under daylight exposure, while the correct fluorescent numerical pattern “123,456” appears under UV light irradiation. In conclusion, the synthetic trichromatic CDs demonstrated significant potential for use in anti-counterfeiting printing applications.

Herein, blue, green, and red fluorescent CDs were successfully prepared using naturally sourced ginkgo leaves as a carbon source through a simple, environmentally friendly microwave method, with doping agents including urea, zinc acetate dihydrate, and NaOH. The prepared trichromatic CDs can be mixed in specific ratios to obtain full-spectrum fluorescence CDs. Characterization results revealed that the QY of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were 13.07, 6.09, and 6.72 %, respectively. The mechanism of fluorescence color difference in trichromatic CDs is mainly attributed to the surface states and size of the sp2 conjugated structural domain. These full-spectrum fluorescence CDs were successfully incorporated into PVA, resulting in PVA multicolor fluorescent films. Fluorescent patterns in various colors were printed via inkjet printing. This study offers fresh insights into the development of multicolor fluorescent displays using CDs, expands the potential applications of biomass-derived CDs, and reveals the significant potential of synthesizing biomass-based CDs for future use. Moreover, there is great potential to increase the QY of CDs in future work.

Changge Sun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuxiang Guo: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Dongxu Mao: Project administration. Yubao Li: Writing – review & editing. Tian Li: Software, Methodology. Ren’ai Li: Resources, Funding acquisition. Qi Wang: Validation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (BK20240685).
